headline: USC's Ground Game Seals Week 1 Win Over Colorado in Close Opener
body: Miller Moss earned Player of the Game honors, but it was USC's ability to establish the run that ultimately separated the Trojans from Colorado in a 27-21 season opener that turned on field position and ball security. Moss completed 29 of 42 passes for 276 yards and three touchdowns, managing the game with poise, yet his counterpart Anderson actually out-gained him through the air with 312 yards and matched his three scoring passes on a higher completion percentage. The deciding factor came in how each team moved the football on the ground.

USC rushed for 161 yards with RB Myers accounting for 86 on 12 carries, giving the Trojans a physical edge that controlled tempo and kept Colorado's defense honest. The Buffaloes managed just 56 rushing yards on their offensive series, a stark disparity that forced Anderson into a pass-heavy diet and left Colorado vulnerable to the clock. Colorado's lone turnover—a fumble lost—came at a moment when field position mattered most, and USC capitalized with the kind of methodical drive that separates wins from losses in September.

What made this contest genuinely competitive was Anderson's arm talent and the receiving corps' ability to stretch the field. Payne's 77 yards and the balance Anderson created kept Colorado within one score well into the final stretch. But without a functional ground attack, the Buffaloes lacked the margin for error that a turnover-free opponent could afford. USC's defense, anchored by Wilkerson's nine tackles, bent but didn't break when it had to, and the Trojans' superior rushing attack bought them the field position and clock management that won the day.
turningPoint: Colorado's lost fumble in the third quarter came when the Buffaloes had momentum, and USC's subsequent scoring drive shifted the game from a track meet into USC's preferred grind, one that favored the Trojans' rushing advantage.

unsungHero.name: Myers
unsungHero.why: His 86 yards on 12 carries established the tone for USC's offensive identity and ate clock in the fourth quarter, yet Moss received Player of the Game largely on volume and efficiency metrics that overlook the run game's critical role in controlling the tempo.
